generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not hope for much these days. Five years have passed since your last visit, and the silence between us has grown as thick as the fog that clings to these rocks at dawn. I write not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of the storm upon my shoulders—both the tempest that howls beyond these walls and the burden of my own heart.

You have no idea, my dear, how the sea has been restless of late. The waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes me think the world itself is coming to an end. I’ve spent these past nights pacing the lantern room, the light flickering like a dying ember, and I’ve not dared to sleep for fear of what might wake me. But tonight, the wind howls with a voice that sounds almost like you, and I cannot help but think of you, Eleanor.

I have not seen you since the day you left, and I have not heard your voice since the last time you called me *father*. I know you blame me for what happened, for the way I failed you. I know you think I was too harsh, too distant, too unwilling to listen. But I have not forgotten the nights I spent watching the horizon, waiting for a sign that you would return. I have not forgotten the way your mother’s eyes would soften when you came to visit

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and I have not heard a word from you since. I suppose it is for the best. You have your own life, your own family, and I have mine—though I sometimes wonder if I have any left.

The wind howls like a wounded beast tonight, and the sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in decades. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night since the day you left, though I do not know if it will be enough to keep the storm at bay. I have grown used to the sound of the waves, to the solitude of this tower, and to the silence that follows your departure. I have not spoken to you in years, and I do not know if I should now, but the storm has made me feel as though I must say something.

I have always been a man of few words, and I have never been good at expressing what I feel. But tonight, as the wind tears at the shutters and the sea crashes against the rocks below, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. I wonder where you are, what you are doing, and whether you still remember the way we used to sit by the fire on winter nights, listening to the stories I told you as a child. I wonder if

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I write with a heavy heart and a mind clouded by the weight of time. These are trying days, and I find myself standing at the edge of the world, where the sea roars and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just my life.

You see, I have kept the light burning these many years, and I have always believed it to be a sacred duty. But tonight, the sea is restless, and the sky is dark with an anger I have never seen before. The waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this old lighthouse. I have seen storms before, but this one feels different—more cruel, more relentless. I fear the sea may not let me keep the light alight for much longer.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written, for I have always believed that words, once spoken, cannot be undone. But now, as the storm gathers, I feel the need to write, to say what I have long kept hidden. I have missed you, Eleanor, more than I can say. I have missed your laughter, your presence, the way you used to call me "Father" with a warmth that I have not felt in years.

I know I have failed you, and I have failed many others. I have let my

**April 15th, 1889**  
**Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now, not with the hope that you will read these words, but with the certainty that if you do, you will know I have not forgotten you. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea rages with a fury I have never seen before. I fear this night may be my last, and I have no words more precious than those I offer to you now.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not known how to reach you. I have tried, in silence, to make amends. I have tried to forgive myself for the way I treated you, for the way I let pride and fear drive the love that should have been my greatest strength. I have not been the father you deserved, and I know that now.

I think of you often, Eleanor. I think of the way you used to run through the gardens, your laughter like the sound of the sea in a summer breeze. I think of the way you looked at me with such trust, such hope, and I have let that hope wither. I have let the world harden me, and in doing so, I have lost the light within me that once shone so brightly for you.

This storm is unlike any I have seen. The sky is black as midnight, and the waves crash against the cliffs

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now by the dim light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside. It’s been five long years since I last saw you, and though the sea has taken many a soul, I feared it might take me before I could speak these words. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write—not out of hope, but out of duty. And perhaps, a little, out of longing.

You were never one for letters, I know. You preferred the silence of your own company, or the company of books. I suppose I should not be surprised. You were always more at home with the written word than with the spoken. But I have missed you, Eleanor. More than I care to admit.

I have kept the lighthouse these many years, though the work grows heavier with each passing season. The storms grow fiercer, the nights longer, and the loneliness of this place more cruel. I have tried, I suppose, to make it bearable. I have read every book in the small library, I have studied the stars, I have kept the light burning through every gale. But no book, no star, no light can fill the void where you once stood.

I have not spoken to you since the day you left. Not a single word. I told myself it was for your own good. That you were too young, too reckless, too… *

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay I have not yet found peace in my own heart. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and though I have not spoken to you, I have not ceased to think of you. The sea, as ever, is a faithful companion, and in its ceaseless rhythm, I find myself recalling the days when your laughter echoed through the halls of our home, and your presence was as constant as the tide.

Tonight, the sky is dark and heavy with a storm that promises to be unlike any we have seen in memory. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, in its fury, rises to meet the heavens with a vengeance. I have spent the day preparing for the worst, securing the lighthouse, checking the lantern, and ensuring that the light will guide the ships through the night. It is a duty I have long embraced, and though it is lonely, it is a duty I do not take lightly.

But I write to you now not as a man of duty, but as a father. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know the silence between us is a chasm as deep as the ocean. I do not know what has driven you away, nor do I seek to pry into the reasons. I have always believed that love, though it may be tested, is never truly lost. And so, I

**From the Lighthouse Keeper at Skerry Point**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1897**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it may not. The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I’ve been keeping the light burning, as I always have, though I fear the storm that’s coming will test even the sturdiest of hearts. I’ve not seen the sky in days, and the clouds hang low as if they know the hour is near.

I write this with trembling hands, for I know I may not live to see the dawn. But I must say these words, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I feel the weight of silence pressing down on me like the tide. You were always the daughter who asked questions, and I was the father who gave answers—until the answers stopped coming.

I remember the night you left. It was a storm like this one, and I had just relit the lamp when you stormed into the house, your eyes full of fire and your heart full of something I could not name. You called me a monster, and I did not deny it. I was a man who had lost his way, and you were the light I could not keep alight. I told you to go, and you did. And I never said goodbye.

But I have not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink still damp from the last candle I lit, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea, that restless thing, has grown more feral tonight, and I fear it may take more than just the light from this tower.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I've kept the lamp burning, not just for the ships, but for the hope that one day you might return. I've spoken to the sea, to the wind, to the ghosts of those who came before me, and none have given me a sign of your return. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel a strange unease. It is not the storm that troubles me—it is the feeling that I may not see the dawn.

You were a spirited girl, Eleanor, with fire in your eyes and a heart as wide as the horizon. I tried to guide you, to teach you the ways of the sea and the patience required to keep the light burning. But you were always restless, always yearning for more than this quiet life. You left with dreams of the world beyond these cliffs, and I could not stop you. I should have. I should have held you closer, spoken more kindly, remembered that you were not just my daughter, but my only child.

I have no regrets, though. Not of you, not of the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a beast outside, tearing at the shutters of this old keep, and I fear it may not be long before it claims me. Yet I must write, for I have not seen your face in five long years, and I cannot bear the silence any longer.

You were once my pride, my light in the darkness of this lonely post. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes burned with a fire I could not understand. You said I was too rigid, too bound to the past, too afraid of change. And perhaps you were right. But I have spent these years watching the sea, learning its moods, its warnings, and I have come to understand that some storms are not of the sky, but of the soul.

I have not written to you in five years, not because I do not care, but because I feared what I might say. I feared that my words would be too harsh, too full of regret, too burdened with the pain of a father who could not keep you safe. I have been a man of duty, Eleanor, and duty has always come before love. But now, as the storm gathers, I see that duty is but a shadow, and love is the true light.

I have not seen you in years, but I have not forgotten you. I have kept your portrait

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write these words with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a banshee outside, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. Tonight, a storm of such fury I have never known is upon us, and I fear that the tempest may claim me before I can speak my final words.

Five long years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not dimmed the ache of your absence. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, but the light has grown dimmer in my soul. I have not spoken to you, not once, and I know that I have not deserved your forgiveness. I have been a man of duty, of silence, of pride, and in doing so, I have lost the one thing that mattered most—your love.

I do not know if you will read this, or if the sea will claim me before you ever receive it. But I must try. I have spent these years wondering if you still remember the nights we spent by the fire, the stories we told, the laughter that once filled this house. I have spent them wondering if you still remember who I was before the sea took everything from me.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even now, in this darkness, I feel its absence. I have tried to be the man you once knew, but I have failed. I have failed you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since I last laid eyes upon you, and yet the weight of your absence feels heavier than the years themselves. I write this with trembling hands, for the storm that brews beyond the cliffs is unlike any I have seen in my time here. The sea roars like a beast awakened from slumber, and the wind howls with a voice that seems to echo with the sorrow of the ages. I fear I may not see dawn.

You were once the light of my life, my little lighthouse keeper, the child who would climb the steps to the tower with a lantern in one hand and a book in the other. I used to say you were born with a compass in your heart, and a lantern in your soul. But time and silence have dimmed that light, and I fear I have been the one to extinguish it.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I do not know if you still remember me, or if the years have worn away your memory like the salt upon these stones. I have tried to write, to reach out, but your letters have always gone unanswered. I do not know if you have forgotten me, or if you have chosen to. I only know that I have carried the weight of that silence like a stone upon my chest.

This storm is unlike any I have faced. The waves rise like mountains, and the wind carries with it a sound that feels

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The sea is fickle, and the wind has a cruel way of carrying things away—letters, dreams, even the sound of your voice. But I must write, for I have something to say, and I have no one else to say it to.

Five years have passed since you left, and in that time, I have come to understand that some things are not meant to be mended. I have tried, Eleanor. I have tried to reach you, to call you back, to offer you the warmth of this lighthouse, the safety of these stones, the quiet of the night. But you were not the one who needed to be held. You were the one who had to let go.

You were young then, and I was old, and I did not know how to speak the words that would have made you stay. I was too afraid of what you might say in return. I was too afraid of what I might say in return. We both ran, in our own ways, from the things we could not face. And so we drifted apart, like two ships on opposite tides.

But now, the storm is coming. A terrible one, they say—winds that will howl like wolves, waves that will crash against the cliffs as if the sea itself is weeping. I have been here for thirty years, and

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not hope for much these days. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and with them, the warmth of your presence has long since faded. I have written to you often, though I know you have never answered, and I fear my words have gone unanswered like the tide that retreats without return.

The storm is coming, and I have not slept since the wind began to howl. The sea, as ever, is restless, and I have seen the sky darken with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have watched the beam cut through the blackness, a solitary thread of light in a world on the verge of chaos. It is not the first time I have faced such a storm, nor will it be the last, but tonight, I feel the weight of the world upon my shoulders like never before.

I have been thinking of you, Eleanor. I have been thinking of the day you left, of the way you stood at the door, your face pale and your eyes filled with something I could not name. You said you could not stay, that the sea was too loud, too cruel, and that you needed to find your own path. I did not understand, and I have never understood why you chose to walk away. Perhaps you thought I would understand, that I would see the truth

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you since the day you stormed out with that foolish notion of marrying that merchant's son. I have tried to write to you many times, but your letters always return unopened, as if you do not wish to read them. I suppose I do not blame you.

The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind has taken on a voice like a thousand mournful wails. I have seen the sky darken with clouds that have not been seen since the time of the great storm that swallowed the ship *Eleanor's Hope*—a ship I once commanded. The waves have grown so fierce that I have had to double the number of lanterns we keep burning, though I fear the light may not be enough to guide the ships through this maelstrom. I have not slept in days, and my hands are raw from the work, though I do not know if it is the work or the grief that keeps me awake.

I have thought of you often, Eliza. I have tried to remember the last time we spoke, the way your eyes would narrow when you thought I was not looking, and how you would sometimes sit in the corner of the parlor, staring at the sea as if it might answer your questions. I have tried to imagine

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I write with a heavy heart. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have often found myself haunted by the silence between us. The days have been long, and the nights have been longer still, with the sea howling like a wounded beast and the wind tearing at the very bones of this old lighthouse. I have spent many hours watching the storm gather, and though I have prepared as best I can, I must confess that I fear for my life. The sea, in all its might, has always been a part of my life, but tonight, it feels different—more furious, more relentless.

I have written this letter with trembling hands, for it is not just a message to you, but a farewell, should the worst come to pass. I have not spoken of my fears before, and I do not know if I should. But I must, for it is the only way I can find peace.

I have spent many hours preparing for the storm, checking the lantern, securing the windows, and ensuring that the light will shine through the darkness. I have prayed for the safety of all who dwell on this earth, and I have prayed for you, Eleanor, more than I have ever prayed for anything else. I have also prayed that the storm will pass, that it will spare us all. But I know the sea does not always listen.

I have not spoken of

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 13th, 1885**

**The Lighthouse, North Sea Coast**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. Five long, silent years since we last spoke, and now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the daughter I once held close, and now, perhaps, to the ghost of a father who has long since lost his way.

You would not believe how the sea has changed me. Once, I was a man of purpose, of duty, of quiet pride in the work I did. Now, I am a shadow of that man, a keeper of the light who has forgotten how to shine it for anyone but the waves. The lighthouse has become both my refuge and my prison, and the wind that howls through the stones is the only voice that answers me.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I know you would call me a fool, a man who abandoned you. But I ask you now, with all the honesty I have left, to understand. I did not leave you. I was taken. The sea, the storm, the endless night—it has claimed more than just my time. It has claimed my spirit, my voice, and the strength to reach out.

The wind is rising now, and I fear for my

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five long, bitter years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and yet the echoes of our last parting still haunt me like the howling winds that sweep across these cliffs. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, but I have not forgotten it. Nor have I forgotten the day you left, nor the sorrow that followed.

The sea has been restless of late, and the skies have darkened with a fury I have not seen since the night you were taken from me. The storm is coming, Eliza—great and terrible, like the storm that tore our lives apart. I have seen the signs. The waves grow taller, the winds more cruel, and the stars seem to weep from the heavens. I do not believe I will see dawn. And yet, I write this letter, for I have something to say before the world takes me.

You were always so quick to judge, my dear. You saw only the man I became, the lighthouse keeper who never spoke of the past, who never called for you, who let the sea claim the silence between us. But you do not know the man I was before the storm, before the grief, before the guilt. I was a husband once, a father, and a man who loved you more than life itself. I did not leave you.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, not with the ease of a man who has seen the dawn of a new day, but with the weight of a soul heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since your last letter, and though I have tried to keep the memory of your words alive, the absence has grown like a shadow that lingers even in the light of day.

Tonight, the sea howls with a fury I have not known since the day I took up this post. The wind, a beast of nature, screams through the cracks in the lighthouse, and the waves, relentless and wild, crash against the rocks with a sound that seems to echo the sorrow in my heart. I fear the storm will not pass without taking something from me—perhaps my life, perhaps my peace.

You were always a woman of sharp words and sharper opinions, and I know that your letters, though few, were never without their barbs. I have read them often in the quiet hours, when the world is still and the only sound is the ticking of the clock and the whisper of the sea. You spoke of me as a man of duty, of stubbornness, of a heart too hardened to feel. I suppose you are right, in many ways. I have spent my life in service, in sacrifice, and in solitude. But I have also spent it in love, though I fear that love has long since been buried beneath the weight

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**

*From the cliffs of Whitby, 1892*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it has not found you at all. Five years have passed since you left, and the world has changed in ways I cannot quite grasp. The sea, once my constant companion, now roars with a fury I fear it has never known before. The storm is coming, and I have no doubt it will be one of the worst in living memory. I write this with the lantern’s glow flickering beside me, a pale reminder of the light I once swore to keep burning for those who sail these waters.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I have not written, either, though I have thought of you often. The nights are long here, and the wind howls like a ghost. I often find myself staring at the horizon, wondering if you ever look that way. I wonder if you remember the lighthouse, or if the sea has claimed it entirely, as it has claimed so many lives.

You were always so curious, Eleanor. I remember the day you asked me why the lighthouse was built. You said, “To guide the lost, like you.” I laughed then, but I think you meant it. You were a child then, and I was a man who believed in duty, in purpose, in the quiet certainty of the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear the winds that howl against these stone walls may not be so kind to me. It is late, and the sea is restless—its fury growing with each passing hour. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though tonight it feels heavier than ever, as though the light itself is burdened by the weight of years unspoken.

Five years have passed since you left. Five long years of silence, of watching the horizon for a ship that never came, of hearing the sea's voice and not your voice in return. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, but it feels smaller now, the walls closing in, the light dimming. I have not spoken to you, not in words, not in letters—though I have written many, and they have all been left unanswered.

I do not know if I will see another dawn. The storm is coming, and the sea has a way of swallowing even the strongest of men. I have always believed in the light, in its power to guide, to protect, to illuminate the darkest of nights. But tonight, I wonder if the light is not enough. I wonder if I have been blind to the darkness that has grown in my own heart.

You were my light, Eleanor. You were the reason I kept the lamp burning, the reason I rose with the dawn, the reason I did not let the sea

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the glass of my window. The sea rages with a fury I have not seen in years, and I fear that the storm may claim me before dawn. Yet, I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I cannot bear the silence any longer.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left this house, the house that once sheltered you, the house that once held your laughter and your dreams. I have tried to forget, but the memories cling to me like the salt of the sea. I have watched the beacon light flicker through the night, and I have wondered if you ever saw it from your window in London, or if you thought of it as a cruel reminder of the life you left behind.

I know you have not written to me, nor have you answered my letters. I have sent them, sealed with my name and the seal of this lighthouse, but they have returned unopened. I do not blame you. You have every right to be angry. I was not the father you needed, and I was not the man you deserved. I allowed the sea to consume my heart, and in doing so, I let you go.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you. I see your face in the flickering light of the beacon, and I hear

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for the storm that brews on the horizon is not the only tempest in my life. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the miles between us are vast, they pale in comparison to the chasm that has grown between our souls.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, a solitary sentinel against the fury of the sea. Each night, I stand at the top of this tower, the light flickering like a heartbeat against the darkness. It has been my duty, my purpose, and my refuge. Yet, as the winds howl and the waves rise, I find myself questioning whether this duty is worth the silence that has come to define my days.

You were once my greatest joy, my light in a world that often felt bleak. I remember the days when your laughter echoed through these halls, and your presence brought warmth to even the coldest nights. But time, and the distance between us, has eroded that warmth. You left, and I did not chase you. I did not understand the pain you must have felt, nor the wounds you carried that I could not see.

I have tried, Eleanor, I truly have. I have written, and I have waited. But your letters never came, and your silence has become a burden I cannot bear. I fear that in my stubbornness, I have only deepened the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left, and though the days have dragged on like the endless hours of a storm, I have never once stopped thinking of you. The sea, ever watchful, has whispered your name to me in the wind, and tonight—tonight—its fury is upon us. I fear I may not see dawn.

You were so young when you left, not yet twenty. I remember the day you stood at the door, your eyes full of fire and frost, and told me you could not stay. “You don’t understand,” you said, your voice shaking. “I have to be free.” And I said nothing, for I could not bear to see the pain in your eyes. I thought I was protecting you, but in truth, I was only protecting myself.

I have kept the lighthouse burning these past five years, as I always have. The light is a promise, a beacon for those lost at sea, and I have never let it go. But sometimes, when the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that shakes the very foundations of the cliff, I wonder if I am not the one lost. I wonder if I have been blind to the light that once shone so brightly in your eyes.

You were my light, Eleanor. I see it now, even in the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I have been of late. I have written many letters to you over the years, but none have ever been sent. I have always feared that the words would not be enough, and that even if they were, you would not wish to hear them. But tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with fury, I feel the need to speak. Perhaps it is the storm that has stirred my heart, or perhaps it is the knowledge that I may not see the dawn.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even when you were gone, I still saw you in the glow of the lighthouse, in the steady beam that cuts through the night. You were my reason for standing here, night after night, in the cold and the rain. I used to tell myself that I was keeping the sea safe, that I was protecting the ships that passed by. But in truth, I was keeping you safe. I was keeping the world from reaching you.

You left me when you were sixteen, with a letter that was more a declaration than a farewell. I have never forgotten the words you wrote: *“I cannot be what you need me to be. You deserve more than a lighthouse keeper’s son.”* I took that as a finality. I thought you had given up on me. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was wrong

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all, for I have not heard from you in five long years, and the silence between us has grown as thick as the fog that creeps in from the sea.

You may not believe me, but I write not out of spite or anger, but from a place of great sorrow and longing. I have spent these years watching the waves crash against the rocks below, listening to the howl of the wind as it sings its mournful tune, and I have come to understand that I have not spoken to you in five years because I feared what I might say. I feared that my words would be too sharp, too full of pain, or perhaps too full of love—love that I have tried so hard to bury beneath the weight of my pride.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the sky darkens with a fury I have not seen in many a year, I find myself unable to hold back. The sea is restless, and the wind howls like a beast in its death throes, and I feel the tremors of the earth beneath my feet as if the world itself is about to end.

I have watched the light burn steady all these years, a beacon in the darkness, a promise that I will not let the world be swallowed by the tide. And yet, tonight, I fear it may not be enough. I fear that even

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very cliffs that hold this lighthouse. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never again. Yet, as the storm gathers, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were a child when I last saw you, a little thing with fire in your eyes and a temper to match. I remember how you would run up the spiral stairs, your small feet pounding against the stone, and demand that I tell you stories of the sea. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since you left this house, and five years since I last heard your voice. I have not spoken to you, not even in the silence of the night, for fear that my words might be the last you ever hear from me. But now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I feel the need to speak—though I do not know if you will hear me.

The sea has been restless all week, and the wind has taken on a voice of its own, howling like a beast in pain. The lighthouse has stood through many storms, but this one feels different. The sky is dark as ink, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have never known. I fear that tonight may be the night I do not rise again. And if I do not, I have no wish to leave this world without saying what I have kept locked away for so long.

I have let the sea take more of me than it should have. I have let the loneliness of this place consume me, and in doing so, I have lost you.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**

**From Your Father,**

**The Lighthouse, Point Gris, 18th of October, 1887**
